coredata.dc:title: CeO2-CDs clusters decorated Co(OH)2 nanosheets for improved photocatalytic ammonia synthesis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huaiwei
Chen, Yifan
Bao, Liang
Ge, Jing-yuan
coredata.dc:description: The green synthesis process of photocatalytic ammonia production has received more and more attentions. Herein, a Z-scheme heterojunction with all-solid-state structures is constructed, in which carbon dots can act as electron transferring mediators. The photocatalytic measurement shows that the modified photocatalysts exhibit much higher activities, in which the ammonia production rates can reach above 232 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 under the light irradiation. The improved catalytic properties can be credited to the significantly increased number of photoinduced oxygen vacancies, the excellent visible-light adsorption abilities and photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiencies for the carbon dots bridged heterostructures. More hydroxyl and superoxide radicals can be simultaneously produced in the composites. This work provides reasonable guidance for applications in photocatalytic ammonia synthesis and a promising construction strategy of efficient Z-scheme photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Non-noble
Heterojunction
Photocatalysis
Ammonia synthesis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns and (b, c, d) partially enlarged curves of the various catalysts.
(a,b) SEM and TEM, (c) TEM-EDS mapping and (d,e,f) HRTEM images of the CeCCOH-2 sample.
XPS detections of the as-prepared samples (a) Ce 3d; (b)Co 2p; (c) O 1 s.
(a) Raman spectra, (b) EPR spectra before and after light irradiation and (c) PL spectra (the inset picture is the TRPL spectra) for the CeCOH and CeCCOH samples.

(a) NH3 production rates of the various catalysts;(b) the cycle performances of the CeCOOH-2 sample; (c) The NH3 yields catalyzed by 1 g of CeCOOH-2 sample under visible (>420 nm) or full spectrum light; (d) AQEs for the CeCOOH-2 sample under monochromatic light (Error bars were calculated by three parallel samples).
(a) Diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra and (b) Transformed Kubelka-Munk function curves (inset image of Mott-Schottky plots) for the various catalysts.

(a) EPR singles for the hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and superoxide radicals (·O2 –) of the Ce, COH and CeCCOH-2 samples under full-spectrum light illuminations; (b) Possible photocatalytic mechanism for N2 fixation under the visible-light irradiation after connection.

head.ce:title: CeO2-CDs clusters decorated Co(OH)2 nanosheets for improved photocatalytic ammonia synthesis

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Environmental Engineering, Hangzhou Dianzi University, Hangzhou 310018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Engineering, Wenzhou University, Wenzhou 325035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green synthesis process of photocatalytic ammonia production has received more and more attentions. Herein, a Z-scheme heterojunction with all-solid-state structures is constructed, in which carbon dots can act as electron transferring mediators. The photocatalytic measurement shows that the modified photocatalysts exhibit much higher activities, in which the ammonia production rates can reach above 232 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 under the light irradiation. The improved catalytic properties can be credited to the significantly increased number of photoinduced oxygen vacancies, the excellent visible-light adsorption abilities and photogenerated electron-hole separation efficiencies for the carbon dots bridged heterostructures. More hydroxyl and superoxide radicals can be simultaneously produced in the composites. This work provides reasonable guidance for applications in photocatalytic ammonia synthesis and a promising construction strategy of efficient Z-scheme photocatalysts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The traditional ammonia synthesis of the Haber-Bosch process requires higher pressure and temperature, which can also consume much of world’s energy [1–6]. The green synthesis process of photocatalytic ammonia production with lower consumption has received increasing attention. During the photocatalytic N2 fixation process, efficiently breaking the NN triple bonds (940 kJ·mol−1) is one of the biggest challenges. This means that the catalysts should have not only stronger adsorption abilities to dissociate the NN bands, but also enhanced redox capacities [7–12].

Cerium-based oxides have abundant surface defects and the reversible electron pairs of Ce3+/Ce4+, which can offer more N2 adsorption active sites and excellent redox abilities [13–18]. However, higher electron/hole recombination rates significantly limit their further application. Therefore, some efforts have been made to improve CeO2-based photocatalyst performance. Crystalline ceria selective growth at the ends of Au nanorods can realize a near-infrared N2 photofixation process [19]. Pu et al. synthesized a Cu2O-decorated CeO2 sample, which efficiently improved the electron-hole pairs separation abilities [20]. Constructing heterostructure photocatalysts with cerium oxide-based materials as the oxidized-type semiconductor are the usual strategy. Some ceria-based heterojunctions, such as CeO2/TiO2 [21,22], CeO2/Ag/AgBr [23], CeO2/CdS [24], CeO2/MoS2 [25], CeO2/LDH [26] and CeCO3OH/g-C3N4/CeO2 [27], have been applied in various photocatalytic reactions.

However, visible-light photocatalysts with heterostructures still face some problems, such as lower charge-hole transfer-separation efficiencies and nitrogen adsorption abilities. All-solid-state heterojunctions with electron transport mediators has been widely used in photocatalytic processes for enhanced electron-hole separation rates [28,29]. Doping noble-metals and transition metals or their oxide nanoparticles in heterojunctions can improve the surface defect structures, visible light adsorption properties and photo-generated electrons transfer efficiencies. Wang et al. reported a type of Z-scheme heterojunction that provided excellent photocatalytic properties. In this system, doped-Ag nanoparticles can act as a charge transfer bridge to further promote the photoinduced electron/hole separation [30–32]. Otherwise, carbon-based nanomaterials have the advantages of more absorption active sites, high conductivity and low cost, which metals or their oxides lack. Therefore, carbon dots (CDs) are ideal materials to replace the commonly used noble-metals as charge transport mediators [33–36]. Heterojunctions with carbon-based electron mediators of BiOBr/CDs/g-C3N4 [37], CdS/CQDs/BiOCl (BiVO4) [38,39] and CeO2-MOH/CDs [40] have been studied, and enhanced visible-light photocatalytic performances were also obtained.

In this research, a type of redox heterojunction photocatalyst with a carbon dot charge mediator was designed using a facial assembly strategy, in which CeO2 nanoparticles with a highly positive VB and Co(OH)2 nanosheets with a negative CB position can serve as reduction and oxidation sites, respectively. The composite samples display efficient catalytic activities during the N2 photofixation process. The modified Z-scheme heterostructures can offer much richer active sites to enhance the electron-hole separation efficiencies.

Cobaltous nitrate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)3·6H2O), ceric nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)2·6H2O), potassium hydroxide (KOH), l-ascorbic acid, potassium sodium tartrate tetrahydrate (KNaC4H12O10·4H2O) and Nessler’s reagent (K2HgI4) were all purchased from Aladdin ltd.(Shanghai, China). The purity Argon (Ar) and nitrogen (N2) were obtained from Minxing Huagong Technology Co., ltd. (Hangzhou, China). All chemicals are analytical grade and used without further purification.

Firstly, 0.434 g Ce(NO3)2·6H2O and 0.291 g Co(NO3)3·6H2O are put into 20 ml deionized water, and 1.12 g KOH are dissolved in another 10 ml water. Then the second solutions are gradually added to the first at 333 K under stirring. The precipitates are centrifuged and washed several times with the deionized water and ethanol. The samples are dried at 333 K for 24 h to obtain original sample (CeCOH). The CeO2 (Ce) and Ce(OH)2 (COH) samples are synthesized via the same procedure except adding pure 0.434g Ce(NO3)2·6H2O or 0.291g Co(NO3)3·6H2O in the first solution, respectively. The CeCCOH samples are prepared via the next hydrothermal reduction process. Typically, 0.044g l-ascorbic acid and 0.2g CeCOH sample are mixed in 30 ml water. Then, the mixture emulsions are transferred into a 40 ml hydrothermal reaction vessel and heated at 433 K for 24 h. The dried sample is CeCCOH-1. Similar synthesis processes of the CeCCOH-2 and CeCCOH −3 are operated except the l-ascorbic acid additive of 0.088g and 0.176g, respectively. The nitrogen content in the various samples is detected accurately, and almost no nitrogen is found. The results guarantee that the nitrogen atoms in the generated NH3 come from the foreign nitrogen gas.

The phase compositions and crystal structures are detected through an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, MinFlex600, Rigaku, Japan). The surface electron structures are confirmed via an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific ltd, USA). The morphology and microstructure are investigated using a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). Raman spectra are recorded on a Renishaw inVia Qontor Raman spectrometer with 532 nm laser excitation. EPR spectra are taken on an A300-10/12 EPR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). DRS curves are measured on a UV-3600 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Photoluminescence spectra (PL) are measured on the FLs1000/FS5 fluorescent spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments ltd., Britain).

To prepare the working electrode, catalysts are dropped onto an indium tin oxide (ITO) glass. Briefly, 5 mg of catalysts are suspended in 0.5 ml ethanol solution with 1 wt% nafion. Subsequently, the slurries are coated on the ITO glass uniformly, and then heated at 333 K in vacuum over 12 h. The reference electrode and electrolyte are the Ag/AgCl electrode and 0.5 M Na2SO4 aqueous, respectively. The electrolyte is bubbled with nitrogen for 20 min before measurement. The Mott-Schottky (M−S) from −1.0 ∼ 0.2 V vs the reference electrode at the frequency of 1000 Hz, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) at 0.4 V vs the reference electrode and photocurrent (I-t) curves at 0.4 V vs the reference electrode are obtained on an electrochemical station (CHI 760E Shanghai Chenhua, China) using as-prepared working electrode.

Photocatalytic nitrogen fixations are performed under the visible or full spectrum light under a 300 W Xenon lamp. Typically, 250 ml ultra-pure water with 100 mg as-prepared catalysts put into in a home-made quartz reactor, and ultrasonic dispersing for 1 h in dark. A circulating water system is used on the outside of the reactor to keep a temperature of about 298 K. The solution is bubbled with pure N2 at a 20 ml·min−1 flow rate for 15 min in dark, and then operated under the light irradiation for 1 h. The NH3 amounts are detected via Nessler's reagent. The experimental details are described in the supplied information. The standard curves are displayed in Fig.S1. After a complete experimental cycle, the sample centrifuges from the remaining solutions and put into the next photocatalytic process.

The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) measurement is the same as the photocatalytic N2 fixation experimental process except that under different monochromatic light wavelengths (365 nm, 420 nm, 520 nm and 610 nm) irradiation using the filters with a bandwidth of ± 5 nm. The values of light intensities are measured by a photo radiometer. The catalytic reactions for determining AQE are carried out in a quartz vessel with the irradiation area of 19.6 cm2. The AQE is calculated as following equation:
AQE =
Number
o f
g e n e r a t e d
N
H 3
∗ 3
Number
o f
i n c i d e n t
p h o t o n s
=
n
N H 3
×
N A
× 3
W × A × t
h × v

Where nNH3 is the molar number of generated NH3; NA and h are the he Avogadro’s constant and Planck constant; W, ν, A and t are the irradiation light intensity, frequency, irradiation area and time, respectively.

The XRD analyses of the as-prepared photocatalysts are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The main diffraction peaks are indexed to the CeO2 (JCPDS #34–0394) and CoOOH (JCPDS #07–0619) phases in the original sample. The CoOOH is transformed into the Ce(OH)2 phase (JCPDS #30–0443) after reaction with the reductant. The local magnified XRD curves according to the CeO2 (111) and (220) planes are displayed in Fig. 1b. After the reaction, the CeO2 phase remains stable in the samples without obvious changes in the characteristic diffraction peaks. Interestingly, subtle shifts in the peaks of the transformed Co(OH)2 phase exist along with the reduction. As shown in Fig. 1(c,d), the differences in the diffraction angles between CeCCOH-1 and CeCCOH-3 corresponding to the (001) and (002) planes are about 0.06° and 0.1°, respectively. Nevertheless, the difference in the (100) plane can nearly be ignored. This illustrates that the lattice constants continuously increase on the (001) plane of the Co(OH)2 phase during the reaction process.

The SEM and TEM photographs of the CeCCOH-2 sample are illustrated in Fig. 2 (a,b). There are two main structures of nanosheets and nanoparticles in the picture. The width and thickness of the regular hexangular nanosheets are about 70 nm and 8 nm, respectively. The main elements are Co and O for the nanosheet regions, while Ce and O dominate the major positions in the nanoparticles (Fig. 2c). The structure with doping CeO2 nanoparticles on the Co(OH)2 nanosheets can be basically confirmed. From the HRTEM images in Fig. 2 (d,e,f), three phases of carbon dots (CDs), CeO2 and Co(OH)2 with lattice distances of 0.21 nm for the (100) plane of graphite, 0.31 nm for the (111) plane of CeO2, 0.27 nm for the (100) of Co(OH)2 and 0.47 nm for the (001) of Co(OH)2 coexist in the composites. These results are also consistent with the XRD results. More importantly, the intimate interface among the three types of nanoparticles is clearly observed, which indicates the generation of the CeO2/Co(OH)2 hetero-structures with the carbon dot charge mediator.

The surface compositions are confirmed via XPS detection in Fig.S2 and Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a shows the Ce 3d XPS image. Four pairs can be found in the curves, and the ν (ν, ν1, ν2, ν3) and µ (µ, µ1, µ2, µ3) peaks are assigned to the Ce 3d5/2 and Ce 3d3/2 states, respectively. ν1 and µ1 are attributed to Ce3+, and the other three, ν and µ, ν2 and µ2, ν3 and µ3, are due to Ce4+ [41]. After the reduction process, almost all the tetravalent cerium ions are transformed into trivalent ions in the CeCCOH samples, and more Ce4+/Ce3+ redox cycles are generated. To evaluate Co stations in the catalysts, XPS spectra of Co 2p are also analyzed in Fig. 3b. Two peaks belonged to Co 2p1/2 and Co 2p3/2 all deviate from the direction of the high binding energy for the reduction catalysts compared to the original. The binding energy of approximately 780 eV for the CeCOH is attributed to the Co3+ ions in the CoOOH phase, and the 781.2 eV binding energy in all the CeCCOH samples is assigned to the Co2+ in Co(OH)2 [42,43]. For the C1s XPS spectra (Fig.S2b), the peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.8 eV are attributed to the CC, CH and CO bonds [44,45]. The increase of CO and CH peak areas in the CeCCOH samples indicate that these composites contain much more oxygen-containing groups, which can help them to be well dispersed in water [46,47]. This also illustrates that the surface structure has essentially changed to a certain extent compared to the normal station of carbon. In Fig. 3c, the peak at about 529.5 eV corresponds to the lattice oxygen (Ol), the 531.2 eV peak is the surface defects or oxygen vacancies (Ov), and the third peak at 532.5 eV is attributed to carbonyl groups or hydroxides (Oa) in the composites [48–50]. An increasing amount of Ov can be generated on the exposed surface through the reduction process. The Ov fractions calculated on the basis of XPS peak areas are occupied 44.5 %, 56.2 %, 57.9 % and 53.7 % of the total oxygen component corresponding to the CeCOH, CeCCOH-1, CeCCOH-2 and CeCCOH-3 samples, respectively.

The catalyst characteristics are further detected through the solid-state Raman spectrum, some of which cannot be clarified through X-ray detections, as shown in Fig. 4 a. The peak with the higher intensity at approximately 460 cm−1 is assigned to the Ce-O F2g stretching mode [51–54]. The peaks at about 500 and 700 cm−1 are attributed to CoOOH and Co3O4, respectively [55,56] . These strong peaks disappear gradually after the reduction process, and Co(OH)2 appears at approximately 625 cm−1 at the same time. Two broad peaks at approximately 1585 and 1360 cm−1 correspond to the G and D bands of the carbon-based materials, respectively. The carbon dots are well dispersed in the CeCCOH-2 catalyst [46,57]. The results prove that l-ascorbic acid can not only play a reduction role, but can also act as a carbon resource.

The Ov states are also investigated via EPR measurements in Fig.S3 and Fig. 4b. All of the samples have an isotropic paramagnetic signal at a g value of approximately 2.003 corresponding to the surface oxygen defects. The EPR signal gradually increased for the CeCOH, CeCCOH-1and CeCCOH-2 samples, and was slightly lower for the CeCCOH-3 in Fig.S3. These results are also consistent with the calculated results in the XPS analyses. The changes in the OV in the catalysts can trigger a shift in the lattice constants. Combining the results of the XRD analysis before, the added Ov are most likely generated on the (001) plane of the Co(OH)2 phase. More importantly, excess photoinduced Ov can be generated under light irradiation, which can improve photocatalytic N2 fixation performances. This phenomenon has also been confirmed in some previous reports [58,59]. Otherwise, the light-induced signal of the CeCCOH-2 sample is much stronger than that of the original. More in-situ Ov can be generated on the reduction samples during the photocatalysis process. The PL spectra are applied to study the photo-induced electron/hole separation efficiencies in the photocatalytic process in Fig. 4c. Significantly, the original sample has a relatively low PL intensity compared to CeCCOH-2. This shows that the separation rate of photo-induced electron/hole pairs has been effectively enhanced for the reduction catalyst. Otherwise, the recombination process of the electron/hole pairs was also effectively limited obtained from the TRPL curves. The CeCCOH-2 sample also has a higher charge carrier density, lower charge transfer resistance and better photocarrier migration efficiency through the photocurrent responses and EIS curves in Fig.S4 [60,61].

The ammonia production rates under light irradiation for the as-prepared catalysts are shown in Fig. 5 (a,b). The original CeCOH sample has relatively low activity under either full spectrum or visible light illumination. The rates reach 232 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 or 162 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 under full spectrum (280–780 nm) light or visible light (420–780 nm) irradiation for the CeCCOH-2 sample, and this catalyst also shows excellent catalytic reaction stability. XRD spectra of the CeCCOH-2 sample before and after the cyclic experiment are displayed in Fig.S5. There are almost no changes in the sample phase after the photocatalytic reaction, which further confirms the reusability of the catalyst. Additionally, ion chromatograph (IC) analyses for the CeCCOH-2 sample under visible light (>420 nm) are applied to prove NH4 + formation (Fig.S6). The reduction sample exhibits the best property, which is also competitive with the most recent studies (Table S1). The enhanced photocatalytic properties are closely related to the band structures and adsorption capacities. The band structures can be greatly optimized by constructing all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunctions. The modified hetero-structure can also offer much richer in-situ Ov and active sites to enhance the electron-hole separation efficiencies, which have been confirmed by some previous reports [12,39,46,62].

Moreover, the produced NH3 yields catalyzed by 1 g sample (CeCCOH-2) in the deionized water under alternative light illumination with intervals of 1 h are shown in Fig. 5c. Almost no ammonia is detected without light or N2 gas flow, implying that the catalytic process is driven by light illumination and that the nitrogen atoms in the generated NH3 come from the foreign nitrogen gas flow. When the reaction time was prolonged to 6 h, the activity of the sample was still well maintained with the NH3 production. The excellent cycle stability and sustainable NH3 production ability ensure that the as-prepared photocatalysts can be used over an extended period. The apparent quantum efficiencies (AQEs) of CeCCOH-2 are also detected under the monochromatic light illumination. As shown in Fig. 5d, the AQEs at 420 nm can reach approximately 0.6 %.

The diffuse reflectance UV–vis spectra and the apparent characteristics for the catalysts are shown in Fig. 6 a and Fig.S7. The various composites of CeCCOH-1, CeCCOH-2 and CeCCOH-1 showed absorption band edges at approximately 430 nm, 460 nm and 530 nm, respectively. Compared with the pure CeO2 sample, they all exhibited enhanced absorption abilities in the visible region. Bandgaps with approximately 3.07 eV and 1.62 eV are indicated for the Ce (CeO2) and COH (Co(OH)2) samples in Fig. 6b. Moreover, their band structures are also studied via the M−S curves, which can explore the nature of the p-n heterojunction. Negative and positive slopes are detected for the Co(OH)2 and CeO2, which correspond to the p- and n-type semiconductors, respectively. On the other hand, the flat-band potential (Vfb) values of Co(OH)2 and CeO2 are approximately 1.08 V (vs NHE) and 0.11 V (vs NHE). In general, the conduction band potential (Vcb) value is less than Vfb by 0.1 V for n-type semiconductors, and the valence band potential (Vvb) value is more than Vfb by 0.1 V for p-type semiconductors [63,64]. The Vcb of CeO2 (0.01 V) and Vvb of Co(OH)2 (1.18 V) are calculated from the Vfb values. When a p-type CH composition is attached to the n-type CeO2 phase in a certain way, the p-n heterostructures will be obtained at the interface. An internal electric field can be introduced due to their different Fermi levels, which can separate the photogenerated charges/holes significantly [65,66].

To determine the photoelectron migration process in the CeO2/Co(OH)2 composites, free radical trapping was also analyzed through EPR measurements, as shown in Fig. 7 a. There are no obvious ·OH signals for the COH sample because of the lower VB value (+1.18 V vs NHE) compared to the potential of OH–/·OH (+2.4 V vs NHE). The ·O2 – signal can be generated in the COH sample due to the much lower position than the potential of O2/·O2 – (-0.33 V vs NHE). In contrast, hydroxyl radicals are detected in the Ce sample rather than the superoxide radicals [67,68]. These provide beneficial conditions for assembling a powerful hetero-structure between CeO2 and Co(OH)2. Based on the abovementioned results, constructing the heterojunction can obviously enhance the generation of ·O2 – and ·OH radicals, which are considered the main reactive species in the photocatalytic process. That is, both photoinduced electrons and holes have the sufficient redox potential as the driving force to overcome the thermodynamic barrier for the efficient production of ·O2 – and ·OH radicals. Hence, with the formation of the Z-scheme heterostructure, the photocatalytic performances of the composites are efficiently promoted compared with the pure components, which is consistent with the above experimental results.

In accordance with the hypothesis, a type II junction heterostructure can be formed in the CeCOH sample (Fig.S8). Under light irradiation, the electrons in the CB of Co(OH)2 can transfer to that of CeO2, and meanwhile the holes in the VB of CeO2 will migrate to that of Co(OH)2. However, the reduction level of CeO2 (0.01 V) was higher than the standard N2/NH3 redox potential (lower than −0.05 V). Therefore, the accumulated electrons in the CB of CeO2 cannot react with N2 to form NH3, which is also consistent with the results that almost no NH3 is generated in this photocatalytic process. The improved photocatalytic performances of CeCCOH samples can be explained by the Z-scheme heterostructure in Fig. 7b, of which only carbon dots can be used as the electron transferring mediator. Many studies have shown that carbon dots are excellent acceptors and donors of electrons [37,69,70]. Specifically, the photo-induced holes and electrons can quickly migrate to the CDs mediator surfaces, and they are redistributed on the VB of the CeO2 phase and CB of the Co(OH)2 phase, respectively. Finally, the excellent oxidation abilities on the VB of the CeO2 phase (3.08 V) and reduction abilities on the CB of the Co(OH)2 phase (-0.44 V) can offer enough energy to achieve the N2 photofixation process.

Based on the advantages of the all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunctions and appropriate band structures between CeO2 and Co(OH)2 [13,42,65], a novel all-solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction with carbon dot electron mediators were assembled through a facile hydrothermal method, aiming at improving the photocatalytic N2 fixation process by regulating the band structures, electron transfer and surface states. There are two main compositions of Co(OH)2 nanosheets and CeO2 nanoparticles for the photocatalysts, and they exhibited excellent photocatalytic performances after CDs decoration due to the more photo-induced oxygen vacancies and higher charge-hole separation efficiency [71]. Furthermore, this type of heterojunction could produce more ·OH and·O2 – radicals simultaneously to further improve the redox abilities. The NH3 production rates could reach above 232 or 162 µmol·gcal -1·h−1 under full spectrum or visible light irradiation without any sacrificial additive agents, which was also competitive with most of metal oxide-based binary or ternary photocatalysts [67,72–74]. The AQEs at 420 nm also reached approximately 0.5 %. Our work may offer a reasonable assembling strategy to further develop the noble metal-free photocatalysts with Z-scheme heterostructures.
Huaiwei Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Yifan Chen: Methodology, Investigation. Liang Bao: Methodology, Investigation. Jing-yuan Ge: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research funded by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation (LY20B010003)

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.12.065.

The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
